text: In connected speech, a sequence like {{c1::next door}} often becomes a softer, faster cluster that sounds like {{c2::nex door}}.
explanation: When three consonants appear close together, native speakers often reduce the middle consonant, especially in casual speech.
usage:
Pattern: cluster reduction / consonant elision
  • Last night sounds like las-night.
  • Next door sounds like nex-door.
spanish: En el habla conectada, una secuencia como 'next door' suele sonar como una agrupación más suave y rápida, casi como 'nex door'.

text: In American English, a {{c1::t}} between vowels may sound like a quick {{c2::flap}} rather than a clear stop.
explanation: This is common in words like water, better, or city, where the sound becomes a light tap in the mouth.
spanish: En inglés americano, una 't' entre vocales puede sonar como un golpe breve en lugar de un bloqueo claro.

text: In some varieties of English, a {{c1::t}} before a nasal or at the end of a syllable can become a {{c2::glottal stop}}.
explanation: This creates a small catch in the throat and is very common in everyday speech.
usage:
Pattern: glottal stop / t-glottalization
  • button → bu'n
  • can't go → ca'n go
spanish: En algunas variedades de inglés, una 't' antes de una nasal o al final de una sílaba puede convertirse en una parada glotal.

text: Function words often reduce to a weak vowel called {{c1::schwa}} in fast speech.
explanation: Words like to, of, and, or can become very short and unstressed, making the sentence sound smoother.
usage:
Pattern: schwa / reduced vowel
  • I can go → I kən go
  • fish and chips → fish n chips
spanish: Las palabras funcionales a menudo se reducen a una vocal débil llamada schwa en el habla rápida.

text: In non-rhotic accents, speakers may add an {{c1::intrusive r}} between vowels to make the phrase smoother.
explanation: This is common in phrases like law and order or idea of it, where a subtle r bridges two vowel sounds.
usage:
Pattern: linking r / intrusive r
  • law and order → law-r-and-order
  • idea of it → idea-r-of-it
spanish: En acentos no róticos, los hablantes pueden agregar una r intrusiva entre vocales para que la frase suene más fluida.
